title: Realizing multi-orbital Emery models with ultracold atoms
authors: Conall McCabe, Jamie Boyd, Kaizhao Wang, Martin Lebrat, Cindy Regal, Adam Kaufman, Ana Maria Rey, Lukas Homeier
abstract: Strongly-correlated electrons in transition-metal oxides give rise to intriguing emergent phenomena, including high-temperature superconductivity in cuprates. While simplified one-band Hubbard models capture some aspects, explicitly describing the interplay of copper and oxygen orbitals -- as in the three-band Emery model -- is essential to capture the full phenomenology of cuprates. Quantum simulators based on ultracold atoms offer a promising route to study such systems in a controlled setting, but realizing realistic multi-orbital Hubbard models remains challenging. Here we propose an optical superlattice architecture that implements the three-band Emery model with ultracold fermions. By combining lattice beams with controllable interference, we engineer orbital degrees of freedom that reproduce key features of the cuprate band structure, while enabling independent control of orbital-dependent interactions and charge-transfer energy. We show that single-particle quantum walks can benchmark the resulting tight-binding model. Using determinant quantum Monte Carlo, we further investigate thermodynamic properties in the undoped regime and find a finite-temperature metal-insulator crossover accompanied by the onset of antiferromagnetic correlations accessible in current experiments. Finally, we apply a Hamiltonian learning protocol enabling to infer effective single-band Hubbard models from experimental realizations of Emery models. Our results provide a practical pathway to simulate multi-orbital Hubbard physics with quantum gas microscopes.
summary: This paper proposes a new way to use ultracold atoms to simulate the complex three-band Emery model, which is essential for understanding high-temperature superconductors like cuprates. By using a specialized bichromatic optical lattice, the researchers show how to control copper and oxygen orbital interactions independently. They demonstrate that the key physics, such as the metal-insulator transition, is reachable with current experimental temperatures. Additionally, they introduce a method to learn effective single-band parameters from experimental data, providing a bridge between complex multi-orbital simulations and simpler Hubbard models.
main_problem: The difficulty of simulating the complex multi-orbital physics of cuprate superconductors, specifically the interplay between copper and oxygen orbitals, using simplified one-band Hubbard models in ultracold atom platforms.
main_result: The authors propose an optical superlattice architecture that implements the three-band Emery model, demonstrating that the metal-insulator crossover and antiferromagnetic correlations are experimentally accessible and that effective single-band parameters can be learned via quantum quenches.
method: The study combines an experimental proposal for a bichromatic optical superlattice with ab initio Wannier function construction, Determinant Quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) simulations, and a Hamiltonian learning protocol based on quantum quenches.
model_or_system: A 2D optical superlattice using ultracold fermionic 6Li atoms in a bichromatic (short and long wavelength) lattice that creates a Lieb lattice geometry, implementing a three-band Emery model with Cu (d) and O (p) orbitals.
key_observables: Charge-ordering temperature, antiferromagnetic spin-spin correlations, incompressibility, Fermi surface shape, and effective single-band Hubbard parameters (t, t', U).
important_parameters: Charge-transfer energy (pd gap) delta_pd, Hubbard interactions Ud and Up, tunneling amplitudes t_pd and t_pp, and the ratio of lattice depths V_S/V_L.
main_assumptions: Assumes that the low-energy physics can be captured by an effective single-band model in the large delta_pd limit and that the 4th band leakage is minimal in the cuprate-relevant regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the cuprate electronic structure and proposed superlattice; Figure 2 details the bichromatic lattice and parameter accessibility; Figure 3 compares the exact potential to the tight-binding model and shows band structure; Figure 5 illustrates the Hamiltonian learning protocol and spectral agreement; Figure 6 shows the gauge transformation for tunneling signs; Figure 9 shows incompressibility and spin correlations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scientific motivation (cuprate physics), proposes the experimental superlattice architecture, provides ab initio parameter extraction via Wannier functions, uses DQMC to investigate thermodynamic properties and phase transitions, presents a Hamiltonian learning protocol for parameter extraction, and concludes with an assessment of experimental feasibility and limitations.
why_interesting: It provides a practical pathway to bridge the gap between ultracold atom simulators and real-world strongly correlated materials by implementing a more realistic multi-orbital model and offering a method to map complex physics back to simpler, interpretable models.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Emery model and its quantum simulation. (a) In transition-metal oxides, such as cuprate superconductors, electronic correlations are dominated by three orbitals near the Fermi energy and are effectively confined to two-dimensional planes. The copper Cu (oxygen O) orbitals are located on the sites (links) of a square lattice, forming a Lieb lattice geometry. Their paradigmatic low-temperature phase diagram shows rich interplay between antiferromagnetic Mott insulators (AF) and mobile charges, including a high-Tc superconducting (SC) dome whose differing size on either side highlights the particle- hole asymmetry. (b) We propose a bichromatic optical superlattice architecture with...

FIG. 2. Optical lattice implementation. (a) Bichro- matic scheme with interfering short lattice (blue) and non- interfering long lattice (red). Center: Optical lattice poten- tial for ϕS = 0.1 and VS/VL = 1.25. The potential minima (purple) form the underlying Lieb lattice. Inset: Probability density of maximally-localized Wannier orbitals on the Cu site (red) and O sites (blue) plotted on a log-scale. (b) Ac- cessible tight-binding parameters varying overall lattice depth and keeping VS/VL = 1.25 fixed with contour lines for Ud/Up (dashed brown lines) and ∆pd/tpd (solid blue lines). The change in band structure, such as |tpp/tpd|, is indicated by the gray background. The absolute tunneling...

FIG. 3. Band structure. (a) Example band structure of the optical lattice potential for VL = 15ER L , VS/VL = 1.25 and ϕS = 0 showcasing three low-lying bands with ∆pd/tpd = 4.2. The dashed lines are the exact structure from the optical lattice potential and the bold lines represent our tight-binding model, where the color indicates the Cu and O character of each band. (b) Direct and virtual tunneling process of single particles initialized in Cu or O sites interfere, which can be used to precisely benchmark the optical lattice parameters. (c) We show the quantum walk of particles starting in three different orbitals. A particle starting on the Cu site (red) is only weakly dispersing due to...

FIG. 4. Finite-T charge and spin correlations. (a) Center: Dimensionless incompressibility ˜κ−1 obtained from DQMC on an 8 × 8 system for Ud = 7, Up = 3.5, tpp = 0.2, t′ pp = tdd = 0.1 (units of tpd), approximately obtained from the parameter path in Fig. 2(b). For small ∆pd/tpd the system remains compressible for all accessible temperatures. As we increase ∆pd/tpd, an incompressible phase appears rem- iniscent of the charge-transfer insulator in Fig. 1(c). Sub- panels: We observe the onset of antiferromagnetic, exponen- tially decaying spin-spin correlations Css(r) at low tempera- tures plotted on a semilog scale (linear scale for |Css(r)| ≤ 10−3). The area of the sites visualizes the...

FIG. 5. Learning effective single-band models. (a) We prepare the system in the ground state of the three- band model, in the presence of small on-site potentials on Cu sites. Releasing those potentials, leads to a low-energy quench, where the system predominantly evolves within the low-energy manifold. Experimentally measuring the terms constituting an effective single-band Hubbard model, allows to learn such effective couplings (up to the absolute energy scale). (b) Using exact diagonalization, we compare the low- energy spectrum of the three-band model to learned, effective single-band models.

FIG. 7. Tight-binding calculations. (a) The nearest- neighbor tunneling tpd in recoil units up to VL = 15ER L . As trap depth increases at constant ϕS, the offset ∆pd grows correspondingly, causing the p-orbital Wannier function to spread further. This expanded spatial extent enables en- hanced tunneling to deeper trap depths. However at larger VL ≥15V R L (not shown) all tunnelings decay exponentially. (b) The perturbative admixing, induced by atomic collisions, of the fourth band with the Emery bands (red for α = d and blue for α = p) for VL = 10ER L as we vary the interference angle. The inset shows the Wannier function of the fourth band which is located at the “fake site”.

FIG. 6. Tunneling amplitudes. (a) Left: The sign struc- ture of tunnelings tpd and tpp has negative (red) and positive (green) amplitudes, arising from the symmetry of the dx2−y2 and px,y orbital. Right: By applying a gauge transforma- tion ˆU that acts on fermions in sublattice B as ˆU†ˆcjB ˆU = −ˆcjB, the sign of tunnelings becomes uniform. Similarly, the signs of the all other tunnelings considered in our tight- binding model are modified accordingly. (b) Sign structure of tunneling amplitudes obtained ab initio from optical lat- tice Wannier functions, consistent with the band structure in cuprates [21] after the gauge transformation ˆU. The tunneling amplitudes in orange are included...

FIG. 8. DQMC sign problem. The sign error for L = 8, as a function of temperature and offset. We encounter a sign problem at low temperatures for ∆pd/tpd < 1 due to our inclusion of finite tpp and Up terms.

FIG. 9. Convergence of DQMC data. (a) The dimen- sionless incompressibility ˜κ−1 at the lowest temperature we consider kBT/tpd = 1/8 as a function of ∆pd/tpd for differ- ent system sizes. (b) The staggered spin-spin correlations for ∆pd/tpd = 1 as a function of distance r. The cutoff of the semilog scale is set to |10−3|, i.e., the scale is linear for ≤|10−3|.
FIG. 10. Emery model superexchange scale. The dashed line indicates the lowest temperature kBT/tpd = 1/8 we con- sider in DQMC.
